title: Formation of gaseous, doubly charged cerium monofluoride CeF$^{2+}$ and its sensitivity to new physics
authors: R. Simpson, C. Zülch, K. B. Ng, I. Belosevic, C. Charles, P. Justus, R. Berger, S. Malbrunot-Ettenauer, A. A. Kwiatkowski, M. P. Reiter, J. Ash, C. Babcock, J. Bergmann, E. Brisley, J. D. Cardona, C. Chambers, A. Czihaly, A. Gottberg, S. Kakkar, J. Lassen, F. Maldonado Milán, A. Mollaebrahimi, V. Radchenko, E. Taylor, A. Teigelhöfer, C. Walls, A. Weaver, P. Weligampola
abstract: Tricationic protactinium monofluoride ($^{229}$PaF$^{3+}$) has been proposed as a candidate for probing physics beyond the Standard Model of particle physics. Since studies with $^{229}$PaF$^{3+}$ require significant experimental advances, we exploit the stable, valence-isoelectronic dicationic cerium monofluoride (CeF$^{2+}$) as a surrogate. Gas-phase fluorinated-cerium molecular ions are formed and identified using the Off-Line Ion Source and TITAN mass measurement facilities at TRIUMF. Quantum chemical calculations are performed on the electronic structure of CeF$^{2+}$, revealing a parallel to that of $^{229}$PaF$^{3+}$. Moreover, these calculations provide estimates on the sensitivity of CeF$^{2+}$ itself to various $\mathcal{P,T}$-odd properties. A brief discourse on the specifics of the quantum control of CeF$^{2+}$ is presented which anticipates future searches for symmetry violations.
summary: This paper presents the experimental production and identification of the doubly charged cerium monofluoride ion (CeF2+) as a surrogate for the radioactive protactinium monofluoride (PaF3+). By using the TITAN mass spectrometer at TRIUMF, the authors demonstrate that CeF2+ can be synthesized in the gas phase using SF6. Theoretical calculations are provided to show that CeF2+ shares an electronic structure with PaF3+, making it a valuable platform for studying parity and time-reversal symmetry violations. The study establishes a foundation for future searches for new physics using controlled molecular ions.
main_problem: The paper investigates the feasibility of forming and controlling the doubly charged cerium monofluoride (CeF2+) ion as a stable, isoelectronic surrogate for the radioactive protactinium monofluoride (PaF3+) to search for physics beyond the Standard Model.
main_result: The researchers successfully demonstrated the experimental formation and high-resolution mass identification of CeF2+ using the TITAN facility and provided quantum chemical predictions for its electronic structure and sensitivity to P,T-odd properties.
method: Experimental methods include ECR ionization, molecule synthesis via SF6 addition in a RFQ cooler-buncher, and MR-TOF mass spectrometry; theoretical methods involve UCCSD(T) and 2c-ZORA-cGHF ab initio calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of gaseous, multiply charged molecular ions (CeF2+, CeF+, and potentially PaF3+) produced and manipulated within the TITAN ion-trap system at the TRIUMF laboratory.
key_observables: Mass-to-charge ratio (m/q), ionization energies, dissociation energies, transition dipole moments, and sensitivity to P,T-odd properties like the nuclear Schiff moment.
important_parameters: Mass resolving power (R > 10^5), ion flux (up to 5 x 10^8 pps), and gas pressures (He buffer gas at ~10^-1 mbar, SF6 at ~10^-3 mbar).
main_assumptions: The use of CeF2+ as a valid surrogate assumes the electronic structure of the isoelectronic Ce2+ [Xe]4f2 and Pa3+ [Rn]5f2 configurations are sufficiently similar for sensitivity estimates.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental workflow from ion production to mass determination; Figure 2 displays charge state distributions; Figure 4 and 5 show high and low resolution TOF mass spectra; Figure 13 illustrates reaction energy landscapes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation (new physics searches), describes the experimental setup at TRIUMF, details the molecular synthesis and mass identification, presents quantum chemical calculations of electronic structure, and concludes with prospects for quantum control.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics as it demonstrates advanced techniques in molecular ion synthesis, mass spectrometry, and the use of molecular spectroscopy to probe fundamental symmetries and precision physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic of the experimental apparatus. (a) Cerium ion production within the Multiple-Charge Ion Source at the Off-Line Ion Source, and separation of the desired ion species according to its mass-over-charge ratio m/q. (b) Formation of diatomic cerium fluoride ions and simultaneous cooling and bunching inside the RFQcb. (c) MR-TOF system for mass determination and subsequent ion species identification. See text for details.
FIG. 2: Typical cerium cation charge state distribution from the Multi-charge ion source with helium

FIG. 3: A high turn TOF spectrum of 140CeF+ and 140CeF+ 2 ions. Here, CeF+ 2 ions have traversed over 306 turns, providing a mass resolving power of 106,000. The TOF is recorded as the elapsed time since the ion bunch has been injected into the MR-TOF analyzer section [see Figure 1(c)]. The second peak in the spectrum, 140CeF+, although being the lighter of the pair, is seen at a later TOF. This is due to the higher number of turns com- pleted, as indicated in the peak label. The number of turns is carefully chosen such that the release point of the ions allows both species to be seen clearly in the same TOF spectrum. This measurement represents the first high resolution confirmation of the...

FIG. 4: A high resolution TOF spectrum (R=296,000) identifying 140CeF2+ after molecular formation with in- coming 140Ce3+ ions. In this 2000 second long measure- ment, the mass range selector [63] is engaged which pro- vided a timed window allowing only the CeF2+ and the 87Rb+ calibrant to pass through while trapped within the MR-TOF analyzer, deflecting all other species.
FIG. 5: Identifying the m/q = 53 peak. A TOF spectrum recorded after a single turn in the MR-TOF analyzer shows a peak corresponding to the m/q of the 140CeF3+

FIG. 6: Energy level diagrams (not to scale) for reactions of various charges states of cerium [row (a)] and protactinium [row (b)] with neutral sulfur hexafluoride. We only include a selected set of products of interest in the diagrams. Activation barriers and transition states are not included in the diagrams. Energy assignments are performed via minimization of the least squares residuals (residuals correspond to 0.3 eV strain on the energy levels) of a collation of ionization (IE) and dissociation energies (DE) from literature and this work (see Table VII and references therein as well as Figure

FIG. 7: Potential energy surface of the electronic ground states of CeF2+ and CeF3+ together with an electron- ically excited state of the trication (CeF3+∗) computed on the level of 2c-ZORA-cGHF. Lines are shown to guide the eye. The wavefunctions of several vibrational states are shown with scaled intensity for visualization indicat- ing possible pathways for quantum control. Exemplary vibronic transitions are shown with vibrational levels in brackets and the Franck–Condon factor f.
FIG. 8: Born–Oppenheimer potential energy curves of the nine energetically lowest electronic states of CeF2+

FIG. 9: Energy landscape (not to scale) of the (X)5/2 (v = 0, J = 5/2) manifold of 140CeF2+. The parameters hΩp and 15h|A|||/7 correspond to the energy difference between the parity states and the hyperfine manifolds, respectively, where h is the Planck constant. ∆EStark corresponds to the Stark shift. The (a), (b), and (c) columns correspond to the cases with zero, weak (∆EStark ≪hΩp), and strong (∆EStark ≫hΩp, h|A|||) external electric fields. In all panes, the vertical direction corresponds to an increase in energy, while the horizontal coordinate reflects the Zeeman sub-levels mF . The upper and lower dou- blets are defined in the caption of Figure 9. The vertical dashed lines indicate hyperfine crossings, where the up- per doublet crosses a F = 2 state in energy; the energy degeneracy results in a much stronger effective coupling.
